data.title: Onboarding skill remediation: pre-flight context accumulation and UX calibration
data.description:
## Context
The DSO onboarding skill, beta-tested against real projects, revealed friction at every layer: non-technical users encountered engineering-specific questions they could not answer, the skill showed no progress indication, dependency installs required repeated manual approval prompts, and hook installation deadlocked on fresh repos. Users had no mechanism to leverage product documentation they already had. This epic restructures onboarding around a pre-flight phase that assesses comfort level, reads an optional user-specified document folder (primarily PRDs and design docs), and auto-detects stack/infrastructure to build a per-dimension confidence context object. Subsequent question routing uses this object — skipping what is already known, confirming what is probable, asking only what is genuinely unknown — for both technical and non-technical users.

## Success Criteria
- A pre-flight phase runs before any onboarding questions: user's comfort level is assessed, an optional document folder is scanned (primarily product/design docs), stack and infrastructure are auto-detected, and a confidence context object is built — routing all subsequent questions (high confidence → skip, medium → confirm pre-fill, low → ask).
- A non-technical user who runs onboarding without specifying a document folder reaches a fully configured DSO environment without encountering engineering-specific prompts (e.g., "npm directly vs. Docker via Makefile"); all such questions are answered using sensible defaults appropriate to their project type.
- The user sees a progress indicator at every stage (e.g., "Phase 3 of 6") and is never uncertain about how much of the onboarding remains.
- From first prompt to final commit, the user receives no more than 6 Claude Code tool-use approval prompts, corresponding to exactly these grouped script invocations: (1) dependency install, (2) scaffold .claude/ structure, (3) config write, (4) initial commit, (5) hook install, (6) final commit. A reviewer can verify this by replaying a fresh-repo onboarding session and counting tool-use approval dialogs.
- Pre-commit is treated as a required dependency checked at start alongside bash and coreutils; hooks are installed as the final step after all other onboarding artifacts are committed; the hook installation commit skips review and test gate verification since the committed hooks are pre-built plugin components, not custom changes; a fresh-repo run completes without any hook-induced commit failure.
- A user who specifies a document folder containing a PRD is asked strictly fewer open questions than in an equivalent run without the folder: at least one question is skipped entirely per high-confidence fact found (stack, app name, WCAG requirements, etc.), and at least one is pre-populated for medium-confidence facts. Verified by replaying onboarding on the same project twice (with and without the folder) and comparing question counts.

## Dependencies
- Architect-foundation non-interactive auto mode epic (being created in parallel) — this epic recommends /dso:architect-foundation --auto to non-technical users; that epic should complete before or alongside this one.
- Coordinate with /dso:doctor (d97a-6bb7) before implementing pre-flight dependency detection to avoid duplicating health-check infrastructure.

## Approach
Restructure onboarding around a pre-flight phase that assesses comfort level, optionally reads a user-specified document folder (primarily PRDs and design docs), and auto-detects stack/infrastructure to build a per-dimension confidence context object. All subsequent question routing consults this object; setup operations are batched into 6 grouped scripts to minimize tool-use approval prompts; pre-commit is installed last; the hook installation commit bypasses review and test gates since hooks are pre-built plugin components.

## Scenario Analysis
- Scratchpad temp file lost on context compaction mid-session (high, runtime): $SCRATCHPAD path lives only in LLM context — compaction causes silent writes; config artifacts produced with no errors but incorrect content.
- Document folder scan stalls on large binary files (high, runtime): No file-type guard — a large PDF exhausts context budget before any questions are asked.
- False-positive stack detection silently writes wrong config for non-technical user (high, runtime): A stray package.json causes node-npm detection at high confidence; non-technical user never sees the stack question; wrong config written silently.
- Hook orphan deadlock on ticket init HEAD change (high, runtime): Dirty working tree prevents HEAD restore; hooks installed on wrong branch appear to succeed but are orphaned.
- Missing commands.validate causes validate.sh to silently pass (critical, configuration): When no test/lint commands detected, key is omitted; validate.sh may gate-pass silently, allowing sprint to close epics without running any validation.
- Worktree CWD mismatch causes review.behavioral_patterns to match zero files (high, configuration): Source dir pattern fails in worktrees; review gate completes with no behavioral coverage.

## Prompt Alignment Findings
- Signal: skill file modifications
- Prior art: CLAM, ARIA, and agentic RAG all converge on confidence-based routing. Approach is well-grounded.
- Behavioral risks to address in implementation: (1) Pre-flight needs an explicit stopping condition after one document pass and one detection pass. (2) HIGH-confidence skips should surface a brief summary line per skip. (3) Phase N/Y counter should be finalized once after pre-flight and treated as immutable. (4) Each command within a batched script must be individually evaluated; failure stops the batch with a reported error.
